title: rblp Development Notes
text: rblp R package implementing BLP (Berry, Levinsohn, Pakes 1995) demand estimation differentiated products. translated pyblp (Conlon & Gortmaker 2020).

title: rblp Development Notes
text: R6 classes: BLPFormulation, BLPEconomy, BLPProblem, BLPResults, BLPSimulation BLPProblem extends BLPEconomy (inherits data setup, adds solve methods) Main files: R/problem.R (solver), R/economy.R (base class), R/results.R (post-estimation), R/formulation.R (model specification), R/simulation.R (data generation)

title: rblp Development Notes
text: Product data must : market_ids, shares, prices, firm_ids, demand_instruments0..N Agent data must : market_ids, weights, plus demographic columns blp_formulation(~ prices + x, absorb = ~ product_ids) fixed effects Integration: blp_integration("product", size = N) Gauss-Hermite Optimization: blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b", method_options = list(maxit = 200))

title: rblp Development Notes
text: pyblp Formulation('0 + prices', absorb='C(product_ids)') → blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ product_ids) pyblp Problem([f1, f2, f3], product_data, agent_formulation, agent_data) → blp_problem(list(f1, f2), products, agent_formulation = f3, agent_data = agents)

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: rblp R implementation BLP (Berry, Levinsohn, Pakes 1995) framework estimating demand models differentiated products. translated pyblp Python package Conlon Gortmaker (2020). package supports: Plain logit nested logit demand estimation Random coefficients logit (mixed logit) agent-level heterogeneity Demographics via Pi matrix interactions Product fixed effects via absorb parameter GMM estimation (1-step 2-step) Post-estimation: elasticities, consumer surplus, HHI, diversion ratios Simulation: generate equilibrium data test estimation Merger simulation: compute post-merger prices welfare effects

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: data 2256 product-market observations across 94 markets. demand_instruments0 demand_instruments19 columns contain 20 pre-computed BLP-style excluded instruments.
code: library(rblp)  # Load the Nevo (2000) cereal data products <- load_nevo_products() head(products[, c("market_ids", "product_ids", "shares", "prices", "sugar", "mushy")]) #>   market_ids product_ids      shares     prices sugar mushy #> 1      C01Q1       F1B04 0.012417212 0.07208794     2     1 #> 2      C01Q1       F1B06 0.007809387 0.11417849    18     1 #> 3      C01Q1       F1B07 0.012994511 0.13239066     4     1 #> 4      C01Q1       F1B09 0.005769961 0.13034408     3     0 #> 5      C01Q1       F1B11 0.017934141 0.15482331    12     0 #> 6      C01Q1       F1B13 0.026601892 0.13704921    14     0

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: simplest demand model. regress log odds ratio δjt=log(sjt)−log(s0t)\delta_{jt} = \log(s_{jt}) - \log(s_{0t}) product characteristics using IV-GMM. price coefficient negative expected – higher prices reduce utility. exogenous characteristics (sugar, mushy) serve instruments prices.
code: # Define the linear model: intercept + prices + sugar + mushy f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  # Create and solve the problem logit_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1), products) logit_results <- logit_problem$solve(method = "1s")  print(logit_results) #> BLP Estimation Results #>   Method: 1S GMM #>   Objective: 2.047932e+02 #>   Optimization converged: TRUE #>   FP converged: TRUE (94 total iterations) #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>  parameter   estimate   se       t_stat  #>  (Intercept) -2.810674  0.109432 -25.684 #>  prices      -11.699731 0.858444 -13.629 #>  sugar       0.048381   0.004208 11.498  #>  mushy       0.043127   0.052717 0.818

what: Example 2: Logit with Product Fixed Effects
title: Getting Started with rblp
text: matches pyblp Nevo tutorial specification. Product fixed effects absorb time-invariant product characteristics (brand, sugar, mushy, etc.), estimate price coefficient. price coefficient -30.42 matches pyblp’s reported value approximately -30.
code: # Absorb product-level fixed effects (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell demeaning) f1_fe <- blp_formulation(~ prices, absorb = ~ product_ids)  fe_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1_fe), products) fe_results <- fe_problem$solve(method = "1s")  print(fe_results) #> BLP Estimation Results #>   Method: 1S GMM #>   Objective: 1.797148e+02 #>   Optimization converged: TRUE #>   FP converged: TRUE (664 total iterations) #>  #> Parameter Estimates: #>  parameter estimate   se       t_stat  #>  prices    -30.420493 1.030311 -29.526

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: random coefficients (RC) logit allows consumer heterogeneity preferences. consumer ii individual-specific taste coefficients: uijt=δjt+∑kxjkσkνik+εijtu_{ijt} = \delta_{jt} + \sum_k x_{jk} \sigma_k \nu_{ik} + \varepsilon_{ijt} νik\nu_{ik} standard normal draws σk\sigma_k captures dispersion tastes. Key parameters: integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3) uses Gauss-Hermite product rule 3 nodes per dimension (34=813^4 = 81 integration points per market) sigma K2 x K2 Cholesky root. Zeros diagonal fix parameter; non-zero values starting values optimization method = "2s" runs two-step efficient GMM (default); "1s" runs one-step
code: # X1: linear demand characteristics f1_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) # X2: random coefficient characteristics (same as X1 here) f2_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  rc_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1_rc, f2_rc),   product_data = products,   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3)  # Gauss-Hermite quadrature )  # Sigma: diagonal matrix with starting values (0 = fixed, non-zero = free) initial_sigma <- diag(c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5))  rc_results <- rc_problem$solve(   sigma = initial_sigma,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 200, factr = 1e7)) )  print(rc_results)

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: Demographics allow observed consumer characteristics interact product characteristics via Pi matrix: uijt=δjt+∑kxjk(σkνik+∑dπkdDid)+εijtu_{ijt} = \delta_{jt} + \sum_k x_{jk} (\sigma_k \nu_{ik} + \sum_d \pi_{kd} D_{id}) + \varepsilon_{ijt}
code: # Load agent (consumer) data with demographics agents <- load_nevo_agents() head(agents[, c("market_ids", "weights", "income", "age", "child")])  # Demographics formulation (no intercept) demo_form <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + income + income_squared + age + child)  # pyblp specification: X1 = '0 + prices' with product FE absorbed # X2 = '1 + prices + sugar + mushy' (with intercept) demo_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(     blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ product_ids),  # X1     blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)                # X2   ),   product_data = products,   agent_formulation = demo_form,   agent_data = agents )  # Sigma (4x4 diagonal) and Pi (4x4, K2 x D) # Starting values near pyblp's converged estimates sigma0 <- diag(c(0.558, 3.312, -0.006, 0.093)) pi0 <- matrix(c(   2.292, 0, 1.284, 0,   588.3, -30.19, 0, 11.05,   -0.384, 0, 0.0524, 0,   0.748, 0, -1.354, 0 ), nrow = 4, ncol = 4, byrow = TRUE)  demo_results <- demo_problem$solve(   sigma = sigma0, pi = pi0,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 1000, factr = 1e7)) )  print(demo_results) # Price coefficient ~ -63, matching pyblp's Nevo tutorial

title: Getting Started with rblp
code: # Compute elasticities for the first market first_market <- logit_results$problem$unique_market_ids[1] E <- logit_results$compute_elasticities(first_market)  # Own-price elasticities (diagonal) cat("Own-price elasticities (first 5 products):\n") #> Own-price elasticities (first 5 products): print(round(diag(E)[1:5], 3)) #> [1] -0.833 -1.325 -1.529 -1.516 -1.779

title: Getting Started with rblp
code: cs <- logit_results$compute_consumer_surplus() cat("Consumer surplus (first 5 markets):\n") #> Consumer surplus (first 5 markets): print(round(cs[1:5], 4)) #>  C01Q1  C03Q1  C04Q1  C05Q1  C07Q1  #> 0.0503 0.0458 0.0944 0.0472 0.0772

title: Getting Started with rblp
code: hhi <- logit_results$compute_hhi() cat("HHI (first 5 markets):\n") #> HHI (first 5 markets): print(round(hhi[1:5], 0)) #> C01Q1 C03Q1 C04Q1 C05Q1 C07Q1  #>   711   643  1435   566  1048

title: Getting Started with rblp
text: Generate synthetic equilibrium data test estimator: Now estimate back true parameters:
code:
# Create a balanced panel: 50 markets, 20 products/market, 4 firms id_data <- build_id_data(T = 50, J = 20, F = 4) set.seed(42) id_data$x <- runif(nrow(id_data), 0, 1)  # True parameters: beta = (intercept=0.5, price=-2, x=0.8) sim <- blp_simulation(   product_formulations = list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x)),   product_data = id_data,   beta = c(0.5, -2, 0.8),   xi_variance = 0.3,   seed = 42 )  # Solve for equilibrium prices and shares sim_results <- sim$replace_endogenous(   iteration = blp_iteration("simple", list(atol = 1e-12, max_evaluations = 5000)) )  cat(sprintf("Price range: [%.3f, %.3f]
", min(sim_results$prices), max(sim_results$prices))) #> Price range: [1.550, 1.715] cat(sprintf("Share range: [%.5f, %.5f]
", min(sim_results$shares), max(sim_results$shares))) #> Share range: [0.00351, 0.15537] sim_problem <- sim_results$to_problem() sim_est <- sim_problem$solve(method = "1s")  cat("True vs Estimated:
") #> True vs Estimated: cat(sprintf("  intercept: true=0.50, est=%.4f
", sim_est$beta[1])) #>   intercept: true=0.50, est=-1.1803 cat(sprintf("  price:     true=-2.00, est=%.4f
", sim_est$beta[2])) #>   price:     true=-2.00, est=-0.9726 cat(sprintf("  x:         true=0.80, est=%.4f
", sim_est$beta[3])) #>   x:         true=0.80, est=0.8334

title: Getting Started with rblp
code:
new_firm_ids <- sim_results$product_data$firm_ids new_firm_ids[new_firm_ids == 3] <- 2  # merge firm 3 into firm 2  merger <- sim_est$compute_merger(   new_firm_ids = new_firm_ids,   iteration = blp_iteration("simple", list(atol = 1e-12, max_evaluations = 5000)) )  cat(sprintf("Mean price change: %.2f%%
", mean(merger$price_change_pct))) #> Mean price change: 7.61% cat(sprintf("Max price change:  %.2f%%
", max(merger$price_change_pct))) #> Max price change:  25.48% cat(sprintf("Mean CS change:    %.4f
", mean(merger$delta_cs))) #> Mean CS change:    -0.0786

Best Practices Differentiated Products Demand Estimation pyblp. RAND Journal Economics, 51(4), 1108-1161.

what: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
text: BLP Demand Estimation Differentiated Products R R implementation BLP (Berry, Levinsohn, Pakes 1995) framework estimating demand models differentiated products, translated pyblp Python package Conlon Gortmaker (2020).

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
text: Plain logit nested logit demand estimation Random coefficients logit (mixed logit) consumer heterogeneity Demographics via Pi matrix interactions Product fixed effects via absorb parameter (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell) GMM estimation (1-step 2-step) Integration: Gauss-Hermite product rule, Monte Carlo, Halton sequences Post-estimation: elasticities, consumer surplus, HHI, diversion ratios Simulation: generate equilibrium data known parameters Merger simulation: compute post-merger prices welfare effects BLP differentiation instruments: build_blp_instruments(), build_differentiation_instruments()

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
code: library(rblp)  # Load Nevo (2000) cereal data products <- load_nevo_products()  # Plain logit: delta_jt = beta0 + alpha*p_jt + beta*X_j + xi_jt f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) problem <- blp_problem(list(f1), products) results <- problem$solve(method = "1s") print(results)

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
code: # Absorb product-level FE (matches pyblp Nevo tutorial, alpha ~ -30) f1_fe <- blp_formulation(~ prices, absorb = ~ product_ids) fe_problem <- blp_problem(list(f1_fe), products) fe_results <- fe_problem$solve(method = "1s") print(fe_results)

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
code: f1_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) f2_rc <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)  rc_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(f1_rc, f2_rc),   product_data = products,   integration = blp_integration("product", size = 3) )  rc_results <- rc_problem$solve(   sigma = diag(c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5)),   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b") ) print(rc_results)

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
code: agents <- load_nevo_agents() demo_form <- blp_formulation(~ 0 + income + income_squared + age + child)  # pyblp specification: prices with product FE in X1, full X2 demo_problem <- blp_problem(   product_formulations = list(     blp_formulation(~ 0 + prices, absorb = ~ product_ids),     blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy)   ),   product_data = products,   agent_formulation = demo_form,   agent_data = agents )  # Starting values near pyblp's converged estimates sigma0 <- diag(c(0.558, 3.312, -0.006, 0.093)) pi0 <- matrix(c(   2.292, 0, 1.284, 0,   588.3, -30.19, 0, 11.05,   -0.384, 0, 0.0524, 0,   0.748, 0, -1.354, 0 ), nrow = 4, ncol = 4, byrow = TRUE)  demo_results <- demo_problem$solve(   sigma = sigma0, pi = pi0,   method = "1s",   optimization = blp_optimization("l-bfgs-b",     method_options = list(maxit = 1000, factr = 1e7)) ) print(demo_results) # Price coefficient ~ -63, matching pyblp

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
code: # Own-price elasticities E <- results$compute_elasticities(market_id = "C01Q1") diag(E)  # Consumer surplus cs <- results$compute_consumer_surplus()  # HHI hhi <- results$compute_hhi()  # Merger simulation new_firm_ids <- products$firm_ids new_firm_ids[new_firm_ids == 3] <- 2 merger <- results$compute_merger(new_firm_ids = new_firm_ids)

title: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products
code: # Create synthetic data with known parameters id_data <- build_id_data(T = 50, J = 20, F = 4) id_data$x <- runif(nrow(id_data))  sim <- blp_simulation(   product_formulations = list(blp_formulation(~ prices + x)),   product_data = id_data,   beta = c(0.5, -2, 0.8),   xi_variance = 0.3,   seed = 42 )  # Solve for equilibrium sim_data <- sim$replace_endogenous()  # Estimate back sim_problem <- sim_data$to_problem() est <- sim_problem$solve(method = "1s")

title: BLP Problem — BLPProblem
text: main user-facing class BLP demand estimation. Inherits BLPEconomy adds solve() interface.

title: BLP Problem — BLPProblem
code: BLPProblem$new(   product_formulations,   product_data,   agent_formulation = NULL,   agent_data = NULL,   integration = NULL,   rc_types = NULL,   epsilon_scale = 1,   costs_type = "linear",   add_exogenous = TRUE )

title: BLP Problem — BLPProblem
text: product_formulations List BLPFormulation objects (1-3). First: linear demand (X1). Second: nonlinear demand (X2). Third: supply (X3). product_data Data frame columns: market_ids, shares, prices, optionally firm_ids, nesting_ids, demand_instruments*, supply_instruments* agent_formulation Optional BLPFormulation demographics agent_data Optional data frame columns: market_ids, weights, nodes* integration Optional BLPIntegration object rc_types Character vector random coefficient types ("linear", "log", "logit") epsilon_scale Scaling epsilon (default 1) costs_type "linear" "log" add_exogenous Whether add exogenous X1/X3 columns instruments

title: BLP Problem — BLPProblem
code: BLPProblem$solve(   sigma = NULL,   pi = NULL,   rho = NULL,   beta = NULL,   gamma = NULL,   sigma_bounds = NULL,   pi_bounds = NULL,   rho_bounds = NULL,   method = "2s",   optimization = NULL,   iteration = NULL,   fp_type = "safe_linear",   W_type = "robust",   se_type = "robust",   initial_W = NULL,   scale_objective = TRUE,   center_moments = TRUE,   delta_behavior = "first",   micro_moments = NULL,   error_behavior = "revert",   error_punishment = 1,   processes = 1L )

title: BLP Problem — BLPProblem
text: sigma Initial K2 x K2 Cholesky root (0 = fixed, non-zero = free) pi Initial K2 x D demographics interaction (0 = fixed) rho Initial nesting parameters (0 = fixed) beta Demand linear coefficients (NA = concentrated ) gamma Supply linear coefficients (NA = concentrated ) sigma_bounds List lower, upper bound matrices sigma pi_bounds List lower, upper bound matrices pi rho_bounds List lower, upper bound vectors rho method '1s' '2s' one-step two-step GMM optimization BLPOptimization object iteration BLPIteration object (contraction mapping) fp_type Fixed point type: "safe_linear", "linear", "nonlinear" W_type Weighting matrix type: "robust", "clustered", "unadjusted" se_type Standard error type: "robust", "clustered", "unadjusted" initial_W Optional initial weighting matrix scale_objective Whether scale objective N center_moments Whether center moment conditions delta_behavior initialize delta: "first", "logit", "last" micro_moments Optional list MicroMoment objects error_behavior "revert", "punish", "raise" error_punishment Punishment scale objective error processes Number parallel processes (1 = parallel)

title: BLP Estimation Results — BLPResults
text: Stores estimation results provides post-estimation methods.

title: BLP Estimation Results — BLPResults
text: problem originating BLPProblem sigma Estimated Cholesky root random coefficient covariance pi Estimated demographics interaction matrix rho Estimated nesting parameters beta Estimated demand linear parameters gamma Estimated supply linear parameters delta Estimated mean utilities xi Demand-side structural error omega Supply-side structural error objective GMM objective value gradient GMM gradient solution hessian GMM Hessian solution se Standard errors nonlinear parameters parameter_covariances Covariance matrix nonlinear parameters W Final weighting matrix optimization_converged Whether optimization converged optimization_iterations Number optimization iterations optimization_evaluations Number function evaluations fp_converged Whether fixed points converged fp_iterations Total fixed-point iterations method GMM method ("1s" "2s") se_type Standard error type

title: Create integration configuration — blp_integration
text: specification Character: "monte_carlo", "halton", "lhs", "mlhs", "product", "grid" size Integer: number draws quadrature level seed Optional RNG seed reproducibility

title: Create iteration configuration — blp_iteration
text: method Character: "simple", "squarem", "return" method_options Named list. Key options: max_evaluations Maximum contraction evaluations (default 5000) atol Absolute convergence tolerance (default 1e-14) rtol Relative convergence tolerance (default 0)

title: Create optimization configuration — blp_optimization

title: Create a BLP Problem — blp_problem
text: product_formulations List BLPFormulation objects (1-3) product_data Data frame market_ids, shares, prices, etc. agent_formulation Optional demographics formulation agent_data Optional agent data frame integration Optional BLPIntegration object rc_types Character vector random coefficient types epsilon_scale Epsilon scaling (default 1) costs_type "linear" "log" add_exogenous Whether add exogenous regressors instruments

title: Create a BLP Problem — blp_problem
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Logit model f1 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) problem <- blp_problem(list(f1), product_data) results <- problem$solve()  # Random coefficients f2 <- blp_formulation(~ prices + sugar + mushy) problem <- blp_problem(list(f1, f2), product_data,                         integration = blp_integration("product", 5)) results <- problem$solve(sigma = diag(3)) } # }

what: rblp: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products — rblp-package
title: rblp: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products — rblp-package
text: Estimate, simulate, analyze demand differentiated products using BLP-type random coefficients logit models (Berry, Levinsohn, Pakes, 1995).

title: rblp: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products — rblp-package
text: rblp package provides tools : Estimating random coefficients logit demand models via GMM Nested logit random coefficients nested logit Joint demand supply estimation Post-estimation analysis: elasticities, diversion ratios, markups Merger simulation counterfactual analysis Consumer surplus computation Optimal instrument construction Micro moments (Conlon Gortmaker, 2025) Data simulation Monte Carlo studies main workflow : Create formulations blp_formulation Set integration blp_integration Define problem blp_problem Estimate problem$solve() Analyze result methods (e.g., results$compute_elasticities()) Translated pyblp Python package Conlon Gortmaker (2020).

title: rblp: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products — rblp-package
text: Berry, S., Levinsohn, J., & Pakes, . (1995). Automobile Prices Market Equilibrium. Econometrica, 63(4), 841-890. Conlon, C., & Gortmaker, J. (2020). Best Practices Differentiated Products Demand Estimation PyBLP. RAND Journal Economics, 51(4), 1108-1161. Nevo, . (2000). Practitioner's Guide Estimation Random-Coefficients Logit Models Demand. Journal Economics & Management Strategy, 9(4), 513-548.

title: rblp: BLP Demand Estimation for Differentiated Products — rblp-package
text: Maintainer: Hyoungchul Kim hchulkim@virginia.edu Authors: Jeff Gortmaker (Author original pyblp Python package) Chris Conlon (Co-author pyblp methodology)
